ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Mee Hoon Kway soup; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta and noodles, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 category for hand-made wheat flour flat noodles served in broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean mee hoon kway; A00LC (pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mee hoon kway or equivalent Singaporean hand-made noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of mee hoon kway soup is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 328 kcal, 15.2 g protein, 11.4 g fat, and 40.9 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Hoon Kway (Soup) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Mee Hoon Kway (Soup), CR ID F05060082. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced exactly. Extended micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean mee hoon kway soup. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: boiled wheat noodles (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021). Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (40.9 g) ÷ 100 = 26.6 per 400 g bowl. The soup broth and protein/fat components (egg, pork) will moderate postprandial glucose response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 378 mg/100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1,512 mg sodium — exceeding 65% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — with insufficient potassium or dietary fibre to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt broth, avoid drinking the soup base, and limit portion size to no more than half a bowl to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour noodles with GI ~65 (medium) and 10.2 g carbohydrate/100 g; a standard 400 g bowl provides ~40.9 g carbohydrate with only 3.0 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 26.6.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl, increase the proportion of protein (egg, pork) and vegetables (chye sim) to slow glucose absorption, and choose wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives when available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 378 mg/100 g raises blood pressure risk; fat content of 2.86 g/100 g includes unquantified saturated fat from pork and ikan bilis; the dish is relatively low in calories (82 kcal/100 g) but the sodium burden is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium broth, limit portion to 200–300 g, substitute minced pork with fish or tofu to reduce saturated fat intake, and add extra leafy vegetables to improve potassium and fibre intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can amplify inflammatory cytokine pathways; sodium at 378 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with fish or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, request extra chye sim or leafy greens for antioxidant benefit, and limit sodium intake by avoiding the soup broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Hoon Kway (Soup), known in Chinese as 面粉粿（汤）, is a traditional Singaporean hawker dish consisting of hand-made flat wheat flour noodles served in a clear savoury broth. The noodles are torn or cut by hand into irregular flat pieces, giving them a chewy, rustic texture that distinguishes them from machine-extruded noodles. A standard bowl is topped with beaten egg (which cooks in the hot broth), minced pork, chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), and ikan bilis (crispy dried anchovies), creating a balanced combination of protein, vegetables, and carbohydrate. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Per 100 g, it provides 82 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 2.86 g fat, 10.22 g carbohydrate, 1.02 g sugar, 0.74 g dietary fibre, and 378 mg sodium. The soup version is lower in calories than the dry version but carries a significant sodium load from the broth, soy sauce, and ikan bilis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on boiled wheat noodles, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the primary concerns are the high sodium content for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, and the medium-GI refined wheat noodle base for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains gluten and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced exactly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F05060082), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled wheat noodles as the closest match, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is high for macronutrients and medium for GI estimation.
notes: Extended micronutrient fields set to -1 (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish, and no equivalent breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singaporean preparation as of 2026. Input record incorrectly classified primary_cuisine as 'western' and ethnic_tags as ['western']; corrected to Singaporean. Halal status is false due to pork content (minced pork, pork broth). GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mee hoon kway soup was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = 65 × 40.88 g carbohydrate ÷ 100 = 26.6.
